behaviors.description: Branch staleness detection correctly identifies branches with commits not in main, older than the configured age threshold, and no open PR.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The failure boundary spans three interacting checks — git commit comparison (commits not in main), age threshold comparison, and PR state lookup. A Unit test of any one sub-function cannot catch a failure in how they're combined. REAL-INFRA: Commit-ahead detection requires actual git history state; a local in-process git repo (e.g., via gitpython or a temp-dir repo) is sufficient to reproduce the failure without real subprocess — an in-process fake reproduces the boundary correctly. REJECTION-GATE: Unit is rejected because the behavior's failure mode is 'all three conditions are ANDed correctly,' which only surfaces when the modules are wired together. System is rejected because neither real network nor real subprocess is required — a local git repo plus mocked PR API is sufficient to expose this failure.
behaviors.test_description: Initialize a temporary local git repo. Create a feature branch with commits not reachable from main. Set the branch last-commit timestamp beyond the configured staleness threshold. Mock the PR API client to return no open PR for this branch. Invoke the staleness detector and assert the branch is flagged as stale-no-pr. Then re-run with the commit timestamp within threshold and assert no flag. Then re-run with an open PR returned and assert no flag.

behaviors.description: Session-start setup emits a stale-work warning with actionable branch context when the current branch is stale.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The failure is in the session bootstrap module invoking the staleness detection module and then routing the result into warning output — a multi-module handoff. A Unit test of session bootstrap alone (with the detector stubbed to always return stale) cannot catch the failure that arises when real detected state is not forwarded to the warning emitter. REAL-INFRA: Git state and PR state can be provided via mocked collaborators; no real subprocess or network is needed to expose this handoff failure. REJECTION-GATE: Unit is rejected because the actionable context (branch name, commit count, age) must be correctly assembled from the detector's output — that assembly is the failure site, not a single function's logic. System is rejected because mocked git/PR state is sufficient to drive the session bootstrap path.
behaviors.test_description: Configure the git context module to report a stale branch (mocked: 3 commits ahead of main, 14 days old) and the PR state module to report no open PR. Invoke the session-start setup routine. Capture emitted warnings and assert exactly one stale-work warning was emitted, that it contains the branch name, commit count, and age, and that no warning is emitted when the same setup is run with the git context reporting a clean branch.

behaviors.description: A local status-classifier function maps computed branch state to labels (`clean`, `stale-no-pr`) deterministically.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The behavior explicitly identifies this as a single local function operating on pre-computed branch state. The failure mode is wrong label output for given inputs — no module boundary crossing is involved. REAL-INFRA: No I/O of any kind. LLM-DEP: No model dependency; the behavior asserts determinism explicitly. REJECTION-GATE: Integration is rejected because the function's contract is 'takes computed state dict, returns label string' — there is no collaborator to wire. The behavior's own text ('local status-classifier function', 'deterministically') disqualifies escalation.
behaviors.test_description: Call the classifier function directly with explicit input dicts covering all label-determining combinations: {commits_ahead: 3, age_days: 14, has_open_pr: False} → assert 'stale-no-pr'; {commits_ahead: 0, age_days: 14, has_open_pr: False} → assert 'clean'; {commits_ahead: 3, age_days: 2, has_open_pr: False} (within threshold) → assert 'clean'; {commits_ahead: 3, age_days: 14, has_open_pr: True} → assert 'clean'. Use parametrize to cover all branches.

behaviors.description: `/kaizen-wip` against live repo and PR state distinguishes 'clean' from 'has commits but no PR (STALE)' in rendered output.
behaviors.justification: REAL-INFRA: The behavior explicitly says 'against live repo and PR state.' The failure mode is in the rendered output produced when the command runs against a real git repo and a real (or real-equivalent) PR API endpoint. A mocked PR client would hide failures such as API shape changes, pagination edge cases, or rate-limit handling. 'Live PR state' disqualifies in-process fakes. LLM-DEP: No model call; the distinction is structural, not scored. REJECTION-GATE: Integration is rejected because 'live repo and PR state' is the behavior's own stated condition — an in-process fake PR state cannot reproduce failures that arise from real API response shapes or real git remote state. The behavior's text provides the concrete disqualifying evidence for Integration.
behaviors.test_description: Run the `/kaizen-wip` command as a subprocess (or via its CLI entry point) pointed at a real local git repo that has a branch with commits not reachable from main and no corresponding open PR on the real (or vcr-recorded) GitHub API. Assert rendered output contains 'STALE' for that branch. Then run against a branch whose commits are covered by a real open PR and assert rendered output contains 'clean'. Use a recorded HTTP cassette or a dedicated test repo to keep the test hermetic while preserving real response shapes.

behaviors.description: Branches with open or merged PRs are not flagged as stale-no-PR false positives.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The false-positive failure arises at the boundary between the PR state retrieval module and the staleness classification logic — the detector must correctly consume PR state and suppress the flag. A Unit test of the classifier alone (behavior 3) already covers the classifier's logic in isolation; behavior 5 is specifically about the retrieval-to-classification handoff. REAL-INFRA: No real network needed — the PR retrieval module can be supplied with a test double returning 'open' or 'merged', which is sufficient to reproduce the false-positive path. REJECTION-GATE: Unit is rejected because behavior 3 already covers the single-function path; this behavior's failure is in whether the retrieval result is correctly threaded into classification. System is rejected because mocked PR state returning open/merged is sufficient — no real HTTP response shape is required to trigger a false positive.
behaviors.test_description: Wire the staleness detector with a mocked PR state client configured to return (a) an open PR, and (b) a merged PR for a branch that otherwise meets all staleness criteria (commits ahead of main, beyond age threshold). Assert that in both cases the detector returns 'clean', not 'stale-no-pr'. Use a third case with no PR (client returns empty) and assert 'stale-no-pr' to confirm the test is not trivially passing.

behaviors.description: Staleness detection remains correct across rebases or squash merges where commit hashes change but branch state should no longer be classified as unPRed stale work.
behaviors.justification: REAL-INFRA: The failure mode is commit-hash-based detection breaking when hashes change after rebase or squash merge. The detection must use git's actual ancestry/reachability logic (e.g., git merge-base, git cherry, or equivalent) rather than naive hash comparison. An in-process fake git repo that only simulates hash presence cannot reproduce the failure — the failure only surfaces when real git reachability commands run against a repo that has undergone actual squash-merge or rebase operations, changing commit SHAs while making the content reachable from main. MOCK-MISS: The module under test must call real git operations to correctly determine reachability; mocking this hides the exact class of bug the behavior is guarding against. REJECTION-GATE: Integration is rejected because no in-process git library faithfully reproduces the commit-graph state that git itself produces after a squash merge — the failure is in how git represents reachability across SHA-diverged histories, which only real git subprocess output reflects.
behaviors.test_description: Using subprocess calls to git, initialize a local bare repo and a working clone. Create a feature branch with two commits. Run staleness detection and assert the branch is flagged. Perform a squash merge of that branch into main (git merge --squash), creating a single new commit on main with different SHAs. Delete or leave the feature branch. Run staleness detection again and assert the branch is no longer classified as stale-no-pr (its content is now reachable from main despite hash divergence). Repeat for a rebase scenario (git rebase main on the feature branch) and assert the same post-rebase classification.
